title: Inference Optimization
lesson:
Inference is the moment a trained model actually does its job — it reads your prompt and writes an answer, one token at a time (a token is a chunk of text, roughly a short word or word-piece). Training happens once; inference happens millions of times, every time a user hits send. So inference optimization means making that answer-writing step faster, cheaper, and able to serve more people at once — without making the answers noticeably worse. If training is writing the cookbook, inference is the restaurant kitchen on a busy Friday night. This lesson is the umbrella view; the neighbouring topics each zoom into one trick.

Why inference is slow in the first place

A language model generates text autoregressively — a fancy word meaning it writes one token, looks at everything so far, then writes the next, like a person typing a sentence and re-reading it before each new word. That has two painful properties. First, it is sequential: token 50 cannot start until token 49 is done. Second, for each new token the model must drag its billions of parameters (its learned numbers) out of memory. Surprisingly, the bottleneck is usually not the math — it is shovelling those numbers from the GPU's memory to its calculators fast enough. We call this being memory-bandwidth bound: like a chef who can chop instantly but keeps running back to the pantry for ingredients.

The two phases: prefill and decode

Every request has two stages. Prefill reads your whole prompt at once — fast and parallel, because all the input tokens are already known. Decode then generates the reply token by token — slow, because each step waits for the last. Most optimizations target one phase or the other, and the table later compares them.

The main levers you can pull

  • Reuse past work. The KV cache stores the model's intermediate notes about earlier tokens so it never re-reads the whole conversation from scratch. Without it, generating token 500 would mean redoing the work for tokens 1–499 every single time.
  • Do the math more efficiently. Flash Attention rewrites the attention calculation so it touches slow memory far less, giving the same answer faster.
  • Guess ahead. Speculative decoding uses a tiny fast model to draft several tokens, then the big model checks them all in one pass — like a junior cook prepping while the head chef approves in batches.
  • Shrink the numbers. Quantization stores weights in 8 or 4 bits instead of 16, so there is less to haul out of memory — directly easing that bandwidth bottleneck.
  • Share the kitchen. Batch inference runs many users' requests through the GPU together, so its expensive parallel hardware is never idle.

A second analogy: the highway

Picture two ways to measure traffic. Latency is how long one car takes to drive the route — what a single user feels as wait time. Throughput is how many cars finish per hour — what determines your server bill. They fight each other: cramming more cars (a bigger batch) raises throughput but can slow each individual car. Real serving engines like vLLM are clever traffic controllers — they use continuous batching, slotting new requests into the GPU the instant an old one finishes a token, instead of waiting for a whole batch to complete. This is the single biggest throughput win in modern serving. Choosing where to sit on this dial is the heart of latency vs quality tradeoffs.

What it looks like in practice

You rarely hand-code these tricks. You pick an engine that bundles them and turn on the flags. Here vLLM serves a model with quantization and big batches enabled:

vllm serve meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct \
  --quantization fp8 \
  --max-num-seqs 256 \
  --gpu-memory-utilization 0.92

That one command quietly switches on the KV cache, Flash-Attention-style kernels, continuous batching (up to 256 requests at once), and 8-bit weights — the whole toolkit, no custom code. To measure whether it helped, you watch two numbers:

TTFT  (time to first token)   = prefill speed,  what feels "snappy"
TPOT  (time per output token) = decode speed,   what sets the words/sec
Throughput (tokens/sec total) = how many users you can serve at once
Prefill phaseDecode phase
Reads the whole prompt in parallelWrites one token at a time
Compute-heavy (lots of math at once)Memory-bandwidth-heavy (lots of fetching)
Sets time to first tokenSets tokens per second
Helped by Flash Attention, batchingHelped by KV cache, quantization, speculation

💡 Don't optimize blind. Measure TTFT, TPOT, and throughput first, find which one hurts, then pull the matching lever. A snappy chatbot and a cheap bulk-summarizer need opposite settings.
mentalModel: Inference is a restaurant kitchen on a busy night. The recipe (training) is done; now it's all about serving fast and serving many. Every optimization is either reusing prep (KV cache), a faster cooking technique (Flash Attention), prepping ahead (speculative decoding), lighter ingredients (quantization), or cooking many orders together (batching).
mistakes: Optimizing for the wrong number. Speeding up throughput (orders per hour) often worsens per-user latency, and vice versa. Decide which one your product needs before you tune — a live chatbot and an overnight batch job want opposite settings.

Forgetting the KV cache eats memory. The KV cache grows with every token and every concurrent user, and it lives in VRAM right next to the model. Long contexts plus many users can run you out of memory even when the model itself fits fine.

Assuming a bigger batch is always better. Past a point, larger batches stop helping throughput and just add latency for everyone. The sweet spot depends on your GPU, model, and prompt lengths — find it by measuring, not guessing.

Treating inference like training. Training is compute-bound and runs once; inference decode is mostly memory-bandwidth-bound and runs constantly. Tricks that speed training (like huge batches) don't automatically help a latency-sensitive serving path.

Expecting quantization or speculation to be free. 4-bit weights can shave quality on hard tasks, and speculative decoding only speeds things up when the small draft model guesses well. Always eval the optimized model against the original on real prompts.

Hand-rolling kernels instead of using a serving engine. Tools like vLLM and serving frameworks already bundle KV caching, Flash Attention, and continuous batching, tested and tuned. Writing your own is almost always slower and buggier.
exercise:
Spin up one small open model (an 8B Llama or Qwen) behind a serving engine like vLLM or Ollama. Send it the same prompt twice: once asking for a 20-token reply, once for a 400-token reply. Record the time to first token (when the first word appears) and the total time for each. Notice that first-token time barely changes, but total time scales with how many tokens you asked for — that's the slow sequential decode phase showing itself.

Stretch goal: Fire 1, then 8, then 32 identical requests at the server at the same time and chart the total tokens-per-second across all of them. Watch throughput climb thanks to batching, then watch each individual request get a little slower. You've just plotted the latency-vs-throughput tradeoff with your own hands.
solution: Hint: Time-to-first-token (TTFT) reflects only the prefill stage — reading your prompt — so it stays roughly constant whether you ask for 20 or 400 tokens. Total time, though, is dominated by decode: one token at a time, so a 400-token answer takes roughly 20x longer to finish than a 20-token one. In the stretch goal, going from 1 to 8 to 32 concurrent requests should push aggregate throughput up several-fold (the GPU was mostly idle at batch size 1), while each request's own tokens-per-second dips slightly. That crossover — total throughput up, per-request speed down — is the core tradeoff every serving setup must dial in.
summary: Inference optimization makes a deployed model fast, cheap, and able to serve many users at once. Generation is slow because it's sequential and memory-bandwidth-bound, split into a parallel prefill and a slow token-by-token decode. The main levers are KV caching, Flash Attention, speculative decoding, quantization, and batching — usually bundled inside a serving engine like vLLM. Always measure latency vs throughput first, then pull the lever that fixes your actual bottleneck.

keyTerms.def: Running a trained model to produce outputs from inputs — the live, repeated step that happens every time a user asks the model something.
Writing one token at a time, feeding each new token back in before producing the next — which makes generation sequential and slow.
The first phase, where the model reads the entire prompt in parallel. It's compute-heavy and sets the time to first token.
The second phase, where the model writes the reply one token at a time. It's memory-bandwidth-heavy and sets tokens per second.
How long a single request takes to answer — the wait time one user feels, often measured as time to first token and time per token.
How many tokens (or requests) the server finishes per second across all users — the number that drives your hardware cost.
When speed is limited by how fast numbers can be hauled from GPU memory to its calculators, not by the math itself — the usual bottleneck during decode.
A serving trick that slips new requests into the GPU the instant an old one finishes a token, keeping the hardware busy and boosting throughput.
How long until the very first word of the reply appears — what makes an app feel snappy or sluggish. Driven mainly by prefill.
